questions.question: What is the strongest first step after an interviewer says the system must handle 10x scale?
options: Identify which assumption breaks first and re-estimate the new bottleneck before redesigning.
Assume horizontal scaling is enough.
Replace every subsystem with a distributed equivalent.
Skip sizing and move straight to multi-region.
questions.explanation: The value of the redesign depends on knowing what changed and why the old design now fails.

questions.question: Why is a 10x change not just a bigger version of the same system?
options: Because a different bottleneck, cost term, or failure mode often becomes dominant at the new scale.
Because 10x scale automatically forces strong consistency.
Because caches stop working at large scale.
Because interviews always require a full rewrite.
questions.explanation: Scale changes what matters, not only how much hardware is required.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of choosing the smallest meaningful redesign first?
options: It targets the broken assumption while reducing migration risk and unnecessary churn.
It guarantees the system will scale forever without more work.
It removes the need for observability during migration.
It means no trade-offs get worse.
questions.explanation: Incremental redesign is usually safer and easier to validate than a full rewrite.

questions.question: Which metric best validates that the redesign actually addressed the new bottleneck?
options: A direct measure of the old bottleneck, compared before and after the change, along with user-visible latency or freshness.
Only total number of servers in the fleet.
The size of the architecture diagram.
The number of migration meetings held.
questions.explanation: You need evidence that the bottleneck moved or shrank, not just that the system changed.

questions.question: Why should a redesign answer include a migration plan?
options: Because many good technical changes become risky or unrealistic if state, clients, or traffic cannot move safely.
Because migration plans only matter for databases.
Because migrations are operational details outside system design.
Because a good redesign never needs rollback.
questions.explanation: A redesign is incomplete if it cannot be introduced safely.

questions.question: What hidden trade-off often appears when pushing more work asynchronous at 10x scale?
options: Throughput can improve, but freshness, backpressure management, and debugging complexity often get worse.
It guarantees perfect ordering everywhere.
It eliminates hotspot risk.
It lowers operational complexity in all cases.
questions.explanation: Async work placement helps scale, but it is not operationally free.

questions.question: If the 10x increase is concentrated in one region, what should a strong answer revisit first?
options: Partitioning, routing, and regional headroom assumptions instead of treating the growth as globally uniform.
Only the company org chart.
The names of the APIs.
Whether to stop measuring cost.
questions.explanation: Localized growth often changes hotspot and failover behavior more than global averages suggest.

questions.question: Why should a redesign answer name one new failure mode introduced by the change?
options: Because the redesigned system often moves risk rather than removing it, and senior answers make that explicit.
Because new failures are impossible after scaling correctly.
Because failures matter only during the initial design.
Because redesign discussions should stay optimistic.
questions.explanation: Ownership means explaining what got safer and what got riskier.
